behaviors.description: The review input is scoped to the lines that changed, not the full content of each modified file.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is in input filtering logic: code reads the entire file instead of just the diff. This is testable by mocking the GitHub API to return a diff, then verifying the code only processes the changed lines. A mock that returns a diff vs. full file will catch a code path that reads the wrong API method. No real infrastructure needed because the bug is in the scoping logic, not in GitHub's API contract.
behaviors.test_description: Mock GitHub API to return a diff with 10 modified lines in a file with 100 total lines. Verify the code only analyzes the 10 changed lines and ignores the remaining 90 lines of unchanged code.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test requires only Unit-level infrastructure because the failure is pure logic (filtering), not API contracts or external behavior.

behaviors.description: Inline comments are attached to the correct positions in the diff view on the pull request.
behaviors.justification: The failure is subtle: diff-relative line numbering differs from absolute file line numbers. A simple Unit mock might assign `line=42` (file line) when GitHub expects `line=5` (diff-relative line). To catch this failure, the test must understand GitHub's actual diff context and review API contract. This requires either a real test GitHub instance or a highly detailed mock that accurately simulates GitHub's behavior. A basic Unit mock risks hiding line-mapping bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test PR in a test GitHub organization with a multi-hunk diff (changes at lines 10 and 50 in the original file). Post inline comments at each changed location. Verify comments appear at the correct diff-relative line numbers in the PR review view, not absolute file lines.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test requires Integration level to accurately verify line-number mapping against GitHub's API behavior. A Unit mock without full GitHub context could miss off-by-one errors or hunk-boundary issues.

behaviors.description: The agent identifies at least one real defect in a pull request that contains a known introduced bug.
behaviors.justification: The failure is in LLM capability: the model fails to identify a bug (e.g., unchecked null dereference, SQL injection, missing error handling). A mocked LLM that returns `findings=['bug found']` passes the test but doesn't verify that a *real* LLM identifies the actual bug in a real code snippet. This is non-deterministic model behavior that cannot be tested without calling the real LLM. Different model versions, prompts, or even the same model on repeated runs may vary.
behaviors.test_description: Create a PR containing a real, introduced defect (e.g., a null pointer dereference, unchecked exception, or SQL injection vulnerability). Call the real LLM-powered review agent on this PR. Verify the agent identifies at least one finding that correctly describes the introduced bug.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: This test must use real LLM because mocked responses cannot verify defect detection capability. The behavior explicitly requires 'real defect' detection, which is an LLM capability boundary.

behaviors.description: Every finding listed in the top-level summary corresponds to an inline comment that was also posted — no findings appear in the summary without a matching inline comment.
behaviors.justification: The failure is in internal logic: the code posts 5 inline comments but only adds 4 to the summary (or vice versa). This is verifiable by mocking the GitHub API and capturing all method calls: post_inline_comment() and post_summary_comment(). Then verify the set of findings in the summary exactly matches the set of findings in the inline comments. No real GitHub behavior or API contract is needed because this is pure synchronization logic.
behaviors.test_description: Mock GitHub API. Instruct the code to generate 5 findings, post 5 inline comments via the mocked API, and create a summary comment. Verify that every finding in the summary comment has a corresponding inline comment in the call history, and vice versa (1-to-1 mapping with no orphans).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit level is sufficient because the failure is in internal finding-tracking logic, not in API contracts or real GitHub behavior. The mock can fully verify the mapping by capturing all API calls.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — reading the diff, posting inline comments, posting the summary comment — completes correctly for a real pull request without manual intervention.
behaviors.justification: The failure is in end-to-end infrastructure and orchestration. Real failures include: real PR metadata causing API rejection, authentication token expiration mid-workflow, rate limiting causing partial failure, or comment formatting not rendering correctly in GitHub. These require real API calls to GitHub (System level). If the LLM's correctness is essential to the end-to-end test (not just mocked), it elevates to Agentic or Workflow level. The phrase 'real pull request' indicates the test must not mock away the PR context or API calls.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real PR in a test GitHub organization containing code with injected defects. Run the full automation end-to-end: fetch the real PR diff via GitHub API, call the LLM-powered review agent, post inline comments to the real PR, and post the summary comment. Verify the process completes without errors, all API calls succeed, authentication is maintained, and the PR's discussion thread shows all inline and summary comments with correct formatting.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: This test requires System level for real API calls and PR metadata. It could escalate to Agentic or Workflow if the test must verify that the LLM correctly identifies defects in the real PR as part of the full orchestrated sequence. Current test description assumes LLM defect detection is verified separately (behavior 3) and this test focuses on orchestration and API calls.
